title: Ensure 'user Connections' Database Flag for Cloud SQL  SQL Server Instance Is Set to a Non-limiting Value

input_row.title: Ensure 'user Connections' Database Flag for Cloud SQL  SQL Server Instance Is Set to a Non-limiting Value
input_row.description: It is recommended to check the user connections for a Cloud SQL SQL Server instance to ensure that it is not artificially limiting connections.
input_row.rationale: The user connections option specifies the maximum number of simultaneous user connections that are allowed on an instance of SQL Server. The actual number of user connections allowed also depends on the version of SQL Server that you are using, and also the limits of your application or applications and hardware. SQL Server allows a maximum of 32,767 user connections. Because user connections is by default a self- configuring value, with SQL Server adjusting the maximum number of user connections automatically as needed, up to the maximum value allowable. For example, if only 10 users are logged in, 10 user connection objects are allocated. In most cases, you do not have to change the value for this option. The default is 0, which means that the maximum (32,767) user connections are allowed. However if there is a number defined here that limits connections, SQL Server will not allow anymore above this limit. If the connections are at the limit, any new requests will be dropped, potentially causing lost data or outages for those using the database.
input_row.audit: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to the Cloud SQL Instances page in the Google Cloud Console by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/sql/instances. 2. Select the instance to open its Instance Overview . Ensure the database flag user connections listed under the Database flags section is 0. From Google Cloud CLI 1. Ensure the below command returns a value of 0, for every Cloud SQL SQL Server database instance. gcloud sql instances describe --format=json | jq '.settings.databaseFlags[] | select(.name=="user connections")|.value'
input_row.remediation: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to the Cloud SQL Instances page in the Google Cloud Console by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/sql/instances. 2. Select the SQL Server instance for which you want to enable to database flag. 3. Click Edit. 4. Scroll down to the Flags section. 5. To set a flag that has not been set on the instance before, click Add item, choose the flag user connections from the drop-down menu, and set its value to your organization recommended value. 6. Click Save to save your changes. 7. Confirm your changes under Flags on the Overview page. From Google Cloud CLI 1. Configure the user connections database flag for every Cloud SQL SQL Server database instance using the below command. gcloud sql instances patch --database-flags "user connections=[0-32,767]" Note: This command will overwrite all database flags previously set. To keep those and add new ones, include the values for all flags you want set on the instance; any flag not specifically included is set to its default value. For flags that do not take a value, specify the flag name followed by an equals sign ("=").
input_row.impact: Setting custom flags via command line on certain instances will cause all omitted flags to be reset to defaults. This may cause you to lose custom flags and could result in unforeseen complications or instance restarts. Because of this, it is recommended you apply these flags changes during a period of low usage.
input_row.default_value: By default user connections is set to '0' which does not limit the number of connections, giving the server free reign to facilitate a max of 32,767 connections.
input_row.additional_information: WARNING: This patch modifies database flag values, which may require your instance to be restarted. Check the list of supported flags - https://cloud.google.com/sql/docs/sqlserver/flags - to see if your instance will be restarted when this patch is submitted. Note: some database flag settings can affect instance availability or stability, and remove the instance from the Cloud SQL SLA. For information about these flags, see Operational Guidelines. Note: Configuring the above flag restarts the Cloud SQL instance.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- The audit can be automated as all necessary information is available via GCP APIs and can be queried using the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) or GCP CLI commands.

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_sql_instance_user_connections_flag_configured

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Use Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) to check the configuration of the user connections database flag for Cloud SQL SQL Server instances.
- Execute the following command:
  ```
  gcloud sql instances describe --format=json | jq '.settings.databaseFlags[] | select(.name=="user connections")|.value'
  ```
- This command retrieves the settings of all Cloud SQL instances and filters for the flag named "user connections".
- Validate that the returned value is `0` to ensure it does not limit the number of connections. 
- **Pass/Fail Criteria:**
  - Pass: If the value of the "user connections" flag is `0`.
  - Fail: If the value is set to any number between `1` and `32,767`, indicating a limitation on connections. 

This allows for an automated audit of the compliance check against CIS controls for GCP regarding Cloud SQL SQL Server instances.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** PARTIALLY_AGREE

- **Manual vs Automated Classification:** The classification as automated is generally correct because the necessary information can be accessed via GCP APIs and queried using the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud). However, the command provided does not specify a particular instance and may not be efficient for environments with multiple instances.
- **GCP SDK Capabilities:** The use of `gcloud sql instances describe` is valid for retrieving instance settings, including database flags.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 7

- **Automation Details:** The command `gcloud sql instances describe` is technically correct for retrieving instance settings. However, it lacks specificity as it does not target a specific SQL instance, which could lead to inefficiencies or errors in environments with multiple instances.
- **Google Cloud SDK Methods:** The use of `jq` for JSON parsing is appropriate but assumes the presence of `jq`, which might not be available in all environments by default.
- **Program Name:** The program name `gcp_sql_instance_user_connections_flag_configured` is somewhat descriptive but could be improved for clarity. It does not explicitly convey the compliance check's focus on ensuring a non-limiting value.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Command Specificity:** Modify the command to specify an instance name, e.g., `gcloud sql instances describe INSTANCE_NAME --format=json`.
- **Efficiency:** Consider using `gcloud sql instances list` to iterate over instances if multiple are present, ensuring each instance is checked.
- **Program Name:** A more descriptive name could be `gcp_sql_sqlserver_user_connections_flag_non_limiting`.
- **Pass/Fail Criteria:** Clarify that a value of `0` is considered non-limiting, but also consider documenting the rationale, as `0` typically means unlimited in SQL Server context.

**CONFIDENCE:** MEDIUM

- The assessment is mostly accurate but lacks some specificity and clarity in the command usage and naming conventions.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:** Modify

- **Modify the command** to specify an instance name for more precise targeting.
- **Improve the program name** to better reflect the specific security check being performed.
- **Consider documenting the rationale** behind the pass/fail criteria to enhance understanding and transparency.
